prerequisites.question: A ball is launched at 20 m/s at 30° above the horizontal. What is the horizontal component of its velocity?
prerequisites.remediation: The horizontal component of a velocity v at angle θ from the horizontal is v·cosθ = 20·cos30° ≈ 17.3 m/s.

prerequisites.question: Which expression gives the vertical component of a velocity v launched at angle θ above the horizontal?
prerequisites.remediation: Resolve the velocity vector: horizontal part is v·cosθ, vertical part is v·sinθ. The angle is measured from the horizontal.

prerequisites.question: An object moves with constant horizontal velocity 5 m/s. How far does it travel horizontally in 4 seconds?
prerequisites.remediation: Horizontal distance = velocity × time = 5 × 4 = 20 m. With no horizontal acceleration, horizontal speed stays constant.

beats.title: Launch it — watch the shape
beats.goal: See that projectile motion traces a symmetric parabolic arc and identify the apex and landing point.

skeleton.text: Here's a cannonball launched at 25 m/s and 50°. Let's watch its full flight.
The highest point — the apex — happens halfway through the flight. It rises to about y = {{projApex(25,50,9.8)}} m.
The total horizontal range is about {{projRange(25,50,9.8)}} m. Notice the shape — symmetric, rising and falling in equal time.
Drag the angle slider. Before you do — what do you think happens to the range when you go above 45°?

beats.title: Split the motion — horizontal vs vertical
beats.goal: Understand that horizontal motion is constant-velocity and vertical motion is free-fall, and the two are independent.

skeleton.text: Let's put this on grid axes. The trajectory is a parabola: y depends on x squared.
Drag this point along the curve. The horizontal spacing between equal-time snapshots is even — constant horizontal velocity. The vertical gaps shrink then grow — that's gravity.
Green = the constant horizontal component v·cos45°. Red = the vertical component v·sin45° — it shrinks to zero at the top, then grows downward. The two act independently!

beats.title: The time equation — when does it land?
beats.goal: Derive the time of flight from the vertical motion alone, connecting it to the symmetric parabola.

skeleton.text: Now think about just the vertical motion: y(t) = (v0 sin θ) t − ½ g t². With v0 = 25 and θ = 45°, the initial vertical speed is {{25*sin(45*pi/180)}} m/s.
Set y = 0 (apart from t = 0) to find the landing time: t_flight = 2 v0 sin θ / g = {{projTime(25,45,9.8)}} s.
So the flight lasts {{projTime(25,45,9.8)}} s, and the range is horizontal speed × time = {{25*cos(45*pi/180)}} × {{projTime(25,45,9.8)}} ≈ {{projRange(25,45,9.8)}} m.

check.text: If you double the launch speed (keeping the same angle), what happens to the time of flight?

beats.title: The range formula and the 45° rule
beats.goal: Discover that range depends on sin(2θ) and is maximized at 45°, then test it with the slider.

skeleton.text: Three launches at the same speed (25 m/s): 30° (blue), 45° (green), 60° (purple). Which one goes farthest?
The range formula is R = v0² sin(2θ) / g. sin(2θ) peaks at 1 when 2θ = 90°, so θ = 45° gives the maximum range. Green wins!
Slide the angle and watch. At 45° the ball lands at {{projRange(25,45,9.8)}} m — the farthest. Any angle steeper or shallower falls short.

beats.title: Uneven ground — launching from a height
beats.goal: Extend to projectiles launched above the landing level and see how the flight becomes asymmetric.

skeleton.text: Now the cannon sits on a 20 m cliff. The ball starts at y = 20 and lands at y = 0 — below its launch height.
The flight is no longer symmetric — it travels farther going down than it rose going up. The vertical equation now carries a constant starting height h.
Solve that quadratic in t. The positive root gives the longer flight time — and therefore a longer range than on flat ground.

check.text: Launching from a cliff, is the total flight time longer or shorter than from flat ground with the same v0 and angle?

beats.title: Putting it all together — design a shot
beats.goal: Synthesize angle, speed, and height to hit a target using everything covered.

skeleton.text: Your mission: hit the target at x = 55 m, launched from this 20 m cliff. Adjust speed and angle.
Start from the guess and adjust. Remember: horizontal range = v0 cos θ × t_flight, and t_flight comes from solving the vertical quadratic with h = 20 m.

check.text: In one sentence: when launching from a height, how does increasing the launch angle affect the flight time?
check.answer: A larger launch angle increases the flight time, because more of the initial speed goes into vertical motion.
